caption: Company overview: 37 years of data, 5,700 employees, 1B+ annual visits, $5B+ invested in research/tech, ~$30B market cap — the scale behind the data moat.
Business-mix shift 2008→2023E: from 100% information/analytics (CoStar 92%) to 55% marketplaces / 45% information — the deliberate diversification into countercyclical revenue.
Revenue by brand 2018-2023E ($1.2B→$2.5B, 16% 5-yr CAGR) with per-brand CAGRs: CoStar 11%, Multifamily 18%, LoopNet 16%, Information Services 20%, Residential 23% off a tiny base.
$40B N. American / >$100B global TAM split by brand: CoStar $4B, Apartments $9B, LoopNet $5B, Ten-X $6B, Homes.com >$15B — Homes.com is the biggest pool and least penetrated.

The research moat quantified: 1,500 researchers, 1,000 developers, 300 canvassers, 70 economists, 50 journalists, 1,000+ drones — the human+capital scale a rival must replicate to match the CRE dataset.
CoStar's land-and-expand engine: 13% 10-yr revenue CAGR by adding asset classes (office→industrial→retail→land→apartments→hotels→residential), against a $4B US TAM led by owners/investors.
Apartments.com since the 2014 acquisition: revenue $86M→$940M run-rate (~11x), traffic 4M→45M uniques (~11x), customers 18K→69K communities, rank #5→#1 — the portfolio's best deal.
Apartments.com unit economics by property size: 100+ units 39% penetrated ($772M run-rate) but 1-49 units only 1% ($60M of a $6B TAM) — the long tail is the $7B headroom.
Apartments.com advertiser-value evidence: 2x more time per visit, 2x higher conversion, 3x more leases vs competitors — the engagement edge that supports subscription pricing.
LoopNet's traffic dominance: ~7.7M monthly uniques vs CREXi ~1.4M — 50x the average CRE-marketplace competitor and 7x the nearest, the network-effect fingerprint of the CRE marketplace moat.
Homes.com 'Your Listing, Your Lead' model: routes consumers to the listing agent and gives free leads, rejecting the buyer-agency lead-resale model that takes a third of rivals' commissions.

Management's moat thesis: 'marketing the property' generated $2.5B cumulative net income 2018-22 vs $1.8B losses for 'marketing the agent' rivals — the slide that frames the Homes.com bet (still loss-making itself).

Management's self-graded capital allocation: estimated IRRs of 17-39% on its commercial deals (CoStar 27%, Apartments 33%, RE Manager 39%) vs ~10% cost of capital — but it omits every loss-making residential/recent acquisition.

New Commercial Real Estate segment map (post Q4-2025 recast): CoStar (incl. Debt Solutions, RE Manager/Visual Lease) + LoopNet (incl. Domain CRE listings) + Other Commercial (Matterport, Ten-X, BizBuySell).
New Residential Real Estate segment map: Apartments.com, Homes.com, Domain Residential, Land.com, OnTheMarket — note Domain is split across both segments (CRE listings vs residential).
Disaggregated revenue under the new 4-line basis, quarterly 2024-2025: CoStar $1,259M, LoopNet $312M, Other Commercial $216M, Residential $1,460M; FY2025 total $3,247M.
Prior-disclosure crosswalk: maps the old geography-era six lines (Multifamily $1,185M, old Residential $218M, etc.) to the new product segments — essential so models keyed to the old lines tie out.
11-year net-new-bookings history 2015-2025: the 2022 surge ($305M), the 2023-24 fade during peak Homes.com spend ($286M→$250M), and 2025's recovery to a new high ($308M).

The 2026 reinvestment agenda: 12 initiatives, +1,031 heads, each with a TAM — incl. CoStar France ($110M+), CoStar New Homes ($200M), Australia Homes.com ($750M), UK Homes.com ($500M+) — the source of the consolidated-margin drag.

Capital-allocation pivot to buybacks: prior $500M program completed in 2025, new $1.5B program (Jan 2026) with $700M planned for 2026 — targeting retirement of ~5% of shares outstanding across 2025-26.
